name: Arrows of Heracles
description: The Arrows of Heracles were no ordinary projectiles; they were imbued with the venom of the Lernaean Hydra, a multi-headed serpent slain by Heracles as one of his twelve labors. Dipping his arrows into the Hydra's poisonous blood transformed them into deadly weapons, capable of inflicting agonizing and incurable wounds. The arrows were renowned for their lethality, capable of felling even immortal beings. These arrows were not merely weapons of physical destruction; they represented Heracles' triumph over monstrous forces and his ability to overcome seemingly insurmountable challenges. Their significance lies in their potent symbolism of resilience, adaptability, and the enduring power of a hero to conquer evil. Beyond Heracles' own use, the arrows played a crucial role in other myths, most notably in the story of Philoctetes, whose festering wound, inflicted by one of these very arrows, initially ostracized him but ultimately proved vital to the Trojan War's success. He was the only one capable of wielding the bow and arrows of Heracles, which were fated to be needed to bring down Troy. The arrows represent a double-edged sword; a source of immense power and potential, but also a cause of suffering and isolation. Their legacy is one of both triumph and tragedy, inextricably linked to the fates of those who wielded them.
subtitle: Hydra-venomed instruments of heroic destiny.
origin: Created by Heracles after slaying the Lernaean Hydra, dipped in its poisonous blood.
currentLocation: Lost to history, possibly buried with Philoctetes or hidden elsewhere.

keyMyths.title: Heracles and the Hydra
keyMyths.description: Heracles' second labor involved slaying the Lernaean Hydra, a multi-headed serpent whose blood was poisonous. He dipped his arrows in the Hydra's blood, making them lethal.

keyMyths.title: Philoctetes and the Trojan War
keyMyths.description: Philoctetes, bitten by a snake, was abandoned due to the stench of his wound, which was inflicted by one of Heracles' arrows. However, an oracle revealed that Troy could not be taken without Philoctetes and the bow of Heracles.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: A Greek tragedy focusing on Philoctetes and his role in the Trojan War.
